planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter for a range of pastel shades from a single seed packet.

directions: Press the seed into the surface and keep it just moist while it germinates over two to four weeks. This seed strain blooms in soft pinks, mauves, crimsons and salmon on tall branching stems, so grow several for the color mix. Shear plants back after the first flush to prompt fresh flowers into autumn.

planting.note: A compact selection suited to the front of a border or a container.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it evenly moist until seedlings emerge. This dwarf form holds deep ruby buttons on shorter, sturdier stems that rarely need staking. Deadhead often to stretch the bloom season from early summer through fall.

planting.note: Grown from division or cuttings to keep the cream-edged variegated foliage true.

directions: Set divisions at the same depth they grew before and water them in well while roots establish. The white-and-green variegated leaves are the main draw, topped by crimson pincushions in summer. Trim off any all-green shoots that appear so the variegation stays strong.

planting.note: Selected for uniform deep red flowers on upright stems.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep it lightly moist through germination. Plants carry rich dark red buttons that bees and butterflies work all summer. Cut stems back by half after the main flush to keep the plant tidy and blooming.

planting.note: Sow direct in a sunny meadow spot or start in fall for natural chilling.

directions: Scatter seed onto firmed soil and rake in lightly, since a spell of cold improves sprouting. This wildflower thrives in poor, chalky ground and needs no feeding. Lilac-blue pincushions appear from midsummer and are a magnet for pollinators; leave some seed heads for finches.

planting.note: Tolerates light shade and moister soils than most scabious.

directions: Sow shallowly where plants are to grow, ideally after a cool period to break dormancy. Unlike its sun-loving cousins this species accepts dappled shade and richer woodland-edge soil. Pale lilac flowers rise on slender stems through summer and self-sow gently if seed heads are left standing.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter for bloom the first summer.

directions: Barely cover the fine seed and keep it warm and moist until it sprouts in two to three weeks. Give plants sharp drainage and full sun; they resent soggy winter soil. Lavender-blue pincushions bloom from early summer to frost when regularly deadheaded.

planting.note: Grown from division or plants to keep this vegetative selection true.

directions: Set plants in well-drained soil in full sun and water until established. This tidy, long-blooming selection produces lavender-blue pincushions almost nonstop from late spring into fall. Shear off faded flowers every week or two to keep the display heavy and the mound compact.

planting.note: A pink counterpart to Butterfly Blue, grown from divisions.

directions: Plant in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun and keep crowns from sitting wet. Soft lilac-pink pincushions rise on wiry stems above a low mound of foliage. Deadhead often and the plant flowers from late spring right through to autumn.

planting.note: A deep-colored selection grown from plants or division.

directions: Set plants in full sun with excellent drainage and water them in well. Saturated purple-violet pincushions cover the compact mound from early summer onward. Regular deadheading keeps color coming until frost.

planting.note: Part of the large-flowered Flutter series, grown from plants.

directions: Plant into free-draining soil in a sunny site and avoid waterlogged winter ground. This series bears unusually large rose-pink blooms on a neat, well-branched plant. Trim spent flowers regularly and it stays in bloom from late spring to fall.

planting.note: Large violet-blue flowers on a compact, floriferous plant.

directions: Give this selection full sun and gritty, well-drained soil for the best performance. Oversized deep violet-blue pincushions float above tidy mounds and draw in butterflies. Deadhead through the season to keep the succession of flowers going into autumn.

planting.note: A very dwarf, front-of-border blue selection.

directions: Set this low grower near the path edge in full sun and sharp-draining soil. Lavender-blue pincushions sit just above a compact cushion of ferny leaves. Snip off the fading heads and the little mound reblooms steadily all summer.

planting.note: A florist's favorite with large, sturdy-stemmed blooms.

directions: Sow indoors under warmth and prick out seedlings once they are large enough to handle. Give plants full sun, good drainage and a neutral to limy soil for the strongest stems. The broad soft-blue heads are held on long stems that are ideal for cutting.

planting.note: The clean white counterpart to Fama Blue.

directions: Start seed warm indoors and grow on in bright light before hardening off. Plant in full sun with sharp drainage and steady, not soggy, moisture. Crisp white pincushions on tall stems bloom through summer and last well in the vase.

planting.note: A long-lived heirloom selection best grown from division.

directions: Divide clumps in spring and replant in full sun with excellent drainage. This classic cultivar carries large mauve-blue flower heads over a long summer season. Cut freely for arrangements, which only encourages more buds to form.

planting.note: An old white-flowered selection, propagated by division.

directions: Lift and split established clumps in spring, setting divisions in well-drained soil in sun. Big creamy-white flower heads rise on strong stems for cutting through midsummer. Deadhead regularly and the plant keeps sending up fresh blooms into early fall.

planting.note: Noted for extra-large, frilled lavender-blue flowers.

directions: Sow warm and grow the seedlings on in good light before setting out. Provide full sun, sharp drainage and a sweet, limy soil for best results. The oversized, fringed lavender-blue heads make outstanding long-stemmed cut flowers.

planting.note: The white-flowered form of the species.

directions: Barely cover seed and keep it warm until germination, then grow on in bright conditions. Site plants in full sun with free-draining, neutral to alkaline soil. Pure white pincushions bloom through summer and are lovely for cutting.

planting.note: A seed mixture ranging from pale to deep blue.

directions: Start seed warm indoors and transplant seedlings once they are sturdy. This old strain flowers in a spread of blue shades on long, cut-worthy stems. Deadhead through the season and the plants bloom generously all summer.

planting.note: An heirloom with deep maroon-black, sweetly scented flowers.

directions: Sow shallowly after the last frost or start indoors for earlier bloom. The near-black, honey-scented pincushions are prized by florists and butterflies alike. Cut stems often or deadhead to keep the plant flowering into autumn.

planting.note: Rich wine-red flowers on tall, wiry stems for cutting.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep once frost has passed and thin to give plants room. Deep claret pincushions with pale stamens rise on long stems ideal for the vase. Keep picking and the plant flowers freely from summer to frost.

planting.note: Warm apricot-peach blooms fading to soft cream.

directions: Direct sow after the last frost or start indoors for an earlier show. This selection blooms in soft apricot and cream tones that suit pastel plantings. Deadhead or cut regularly to prolong flowering through the warm months.

planting.note: Soft salmon-pink flowers on a tall cutting annual.

directions: Sow shallowly into warm soil and thin seedlings once they crowd. Gentle salmon-pink pincushions bloom on long stems that hold well when cut. Steady deadheading keeps the plant producing until the first hard frost.

planting.note: Dark burgundy-black, strongly honey-scented flowers.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep after frost, or begin indoors for an early start. The velvety near-black flowers carry a rich honey fragrance that pollinators love. Cut or deadhead frequently and the plant blooms right up to frost.

planting.note: Very dark maroon blooms on especially tall stems.

directions: Sow shallowly once soil warms and support the tall stems in windy spots. Deep maroon-black pincushions with white flecks stand out in the cutting garden. Keep harvesting and the plant flowers abundantly through the season.

planting.note: Free-flowering and short-lived, but self-sows readily.

directions: Sow seed shallowly on well-drained soil in a sunny, open spot. This airy species produces a haze of small primrose-yellow pincushions on branching stems. It blooms for months and gently reseeds, so leave a few heads to stand.

planting.note: A dwarf, cushion-forming scabious for the rock garden.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep it warm until seedlings emerge. This compact alpine forms a neat mound of finely cut foliage topped by lavender-blue buttons. Give it gritty, sharply drained soil and full sun; deadhead to extend the long bloom.

planting.note: A silvery, grassy-leaved species for hot, dry sites.

directions: Start seed warm and grow the seedlings on in bright light and gritty compost. This low plant forms silvery, grass-like foliage carrying soft mauve-pink pincushions. Plant it in lean, sharply drained soil in full sun and avoid overwatering.

planting.note: A compact alpine species with glossy leaves.

directions: Sow warm and prick out seedlings once they are big enough to handle. This tidy dwarf carries lilac-pink pincushions above shiny green foliage. Give it sharp drainage and full sun; it suits rock gardens and gravel plantings.

planting.note: A woody-based Mediterranean species for mild, dry gardens.

directions: Start seed warm and grow on the young plants in free-draining compost. This shrubby scabious forms a rounded, silvery-leaved bush topped with soft lilac flowers. It needs a hot, sheltered spot with sharp drainage and only survives frost in mild climates.

planting.note: Grown as much for its papery seed globes as its flowers.

directions: Sow seed a quarter inch deep after the last frost and thin to give plants space. Pale blue flowers give way to spherical bronze seed heads made of papery, star-marked cups. Cut the globes when firm for everlasting arrangements, or leave a few to self-sow.

planting.note: Selected for uniform, ball-shaped seed heads.

directions: Direct sow after frost and thin seedlings to a hand's width apart. Small lilac flowers are quickly followed by neat round seed globes marked with dark stars. Harvest the papery balls when they firm up for dried bouquets and crafts.

planting.note: A German selection prized for its drumstick seed heads.

directions: Sow shallowly once frost danger passes and thin the young plants. Pale blue blooms mature into bronze-green globes covered in tiny star-shaped cups. Cut the spheres when firm and hang them to dry for lasting displays.

planting.note: A woodland-edge species that accepts some shade.

directions: Sow seed shallowly where plants are to grow, ideally after a cold spell. This tough species tolerates dappled shade and average garden soil. Reddish-purple pincushions appear through summer and feed a range of pollinating insects.

planting.note: Divide clumps or set rooted cuttings out in spring after the ground thaws.

directions: Plant divisions at the depth they grew, spacing them so the silver mats knit together within a season. Give this cultivar afternoon shade and steady moisture to keep the foliage from scorching. Pinkish-mauve flowers rise above the nearly solid silver leaves in late spring.

planting.note: Set out divisions or cuttings in spring as growth resumes.

directions: Tuck rooted pieces into cool, moist ground and firm the soil around the stem bases. Trim back leggy growth after bloom to renew the silver carpet. Look for pure white flowers set against frosted-silver leaves edged in green through late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring once frost danger eases.

directions: Space plants about a foot apart in shade and keep them evenly watered while roots establish. Shear spent stems to encourage a fresh mound of silvery leaves. Soft salmon-pink flowers appear over the pewter foliage from late spring onward.

planting.note: Set rooted plants out in spring in a shaded bed.

directions: Plant in loose, moisture-retentive soil and mulch lightly to keep roots cool. Cut the mat back by half after the main bloom to prompt a second flush. Large deep purple flowers stand out above silver-centered leaves in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions or plugs in spring after the soil warms slightly.

directions: Set plants in dappled shade with rich, well-drained soil and water during dry spells. This vigorous, mildew-resistant cultivar spreads steadily, so give it room. Bright orchid-pink flowers cover the frosty silver-and-green leaves from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a spot with shelter from harsh midday sun.

directions: Give this gold-leaved form bright, filtered light to hold color without burning the tender foliage. Keep it consistently moist, as the yellow leaves scorch more easily than green types. Pink flowers rise over the chartreuse-gold, silver-striped leaves in late spring.

planting.note: Divide and replant in spring for quick shade groundcover.

directions: Plant this vigorous grower where it can ramble beneath shrubs, spacing about a foot apart. Cut it back after flowering to tidy the mat and refresh the leaves. Rosy-purple flowers open above green leaves with a broad silver central stripe from late spring.

planting.note: Plant rooted divisions in spring in a bright, sheltered shade bed.

directions: Site this golden-leaved cultivar in light shade so the foliage stays bright without scorching. Keep the soil evenly moist and shear after bloom to renew growth. White flowers contrast with the yellow-green leaves in late spring.

planting.note: Set out plants in spring for a colorful shade carpet.

directions: Plant in humus-rich soil in partial shade and water through dry weather. Trim spent flower stems to keep the striking foliage looking fresh. Purple-pink flowers appear over leaves marked in green, chartreuse, and silver from late spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring once soil is workable.

directions: Space plants about a foot apart in cool shade with moist, well-drained soil. Shear back midseason to keep the mat compact. Pale shell-pink flowers open above green leaves with a silver central band in late spring.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings out in spring in shade.

directions: Set plants in moisture-retentive soil under trees and keep them from drying out. Cut back after the main bloom to encourage a repeat show. Rosy-red flowers stand above the frosted silver leaves through late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a lightly shaded, evenly moist bed.

directions: Give this larger cultivar rich, moist soil and shelter from hot afternoon sun. Water during droughts, since the pale leaves show stress quickly. Purple flowers rise over silvery leaves with thin green margins from late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in bright shade to hold leaf color.

directions: Site this solid-gold form in filtered light where the foliage will not burn. Keep the soil steadily moist and remove any scorched leaves. Purple-pink flowers appear over the plain golden-yellow leaves in late spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sheltered shade bed.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, moist soil in partial shade and water in dry spells. Shear back after bloom to keep the mat full. Purple-pink flowers top the tricolor leaves of gold, green, and silver in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Set out plants in spring in dappled shade.

directions: Plant in cool, moist soil and give bright shade to keep the variegation vivid. Trim spent stems to refresh the foliage through summer. Soft pink flowers appear over green leaves splashed with gold and cream in late spring.

planting.note: Divide and replant in spring for a shady groundcover.

directions: Plant divisions about a foot apart in moist, well-drained shade. Cut back after flowering to keep the mat dense and tidy. Pure white flowers open over green leaves striped with silver in late spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; this clumping form stays put unlike the species.

directions: Plant this well-behaved, non-running selection in dry to average shade and space about a foot apart. Unlike the wild species it forms tidy clumps rather than aggressive runners. Yellow flowers rise among the narrow silver-streaked leaves in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade where its spreading habit is welcome.

directions: Use this vigorous trailing form to cover dry shade quickly, but keep it away from natural areas where it can spread. Cut back hard if it outgrows its space. Yellow flowers appear in late spring over silver-marbled leaves that flush purple in cold weather.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in shade; contain its runners.

directions: Plant in shade to brighten dark corners, spacing plants to let the silver foliage merge. This running form spreads fast, so use it where it can be kept in bounds. Yellow hooded flowers open in late spring above green leaves broadly marked with silver.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a trailing shade groundcover.

directions: Plant this low, trailing selection in dry to moderate shade and let it cascade over slopes or wall edges. It roots as it runs, so give it room or trim it back. Yellow flowers appear over strongly silvered leaves in late spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich shade.

directions: Set clumps in cool, fertile woodland soil and keep them evenly moist through the season. This white-flowered form stays as a tidy clump rather than running. Creamy-white flowers gather along the upright stems in late spring over broad green leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring in gritty, well-drained soil in sun or light shade.

directions: Sow seed cool and give this alpine species sharp drainage in rock gardens or raised beds. It tolerates more sun and drier ground than the woodland deadnettles. Comparatively large pink to purple-red flowers open in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Direct sow in cool soil in early spring or autumn.

directions: Sow onto cool, moist ground and barely cover, since this cool-season annual germinates best in fall and early spring. It flowers very early and feeds emerging bees, but self-sows heavily and can become weedy. Clasping rounded leaves ring the stems below slender pinkish-purple flowers from late winter into spring.

planting.note: Plant nursery stock or rooted cuttings after frost danger passes.

directions: Set the crown just at soil level in lean, fast-draining ground and mulch with grit rather than bark. Prized for its deep violet-blue spikes, this compact form makes a tidy low hedge. Cut stems for drying just as the lowest buds open for the richest color and scent.

planting.note: Transplant after the last frost into a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Space plants for airflow to prevent fungal problems in humid summers. This early, reliable form carries lavender-blue flowers on a neat mound and tolerates cold well. Trim back by a third in spring once new growth appears, never cutting into old bare wood.

planting.note: Plant out after frost into gritty, alkaline soil in full sun.

directions: Give this rosy-pink form the same lean, dry conditions as the blue lavenders. The soft mauve-pink spikes pair well with silver foliage plants. Deadhead after flowering and shear lightly to keep the bush compact.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into a warm, sharply drained spot.

directions: This miniature white lavender suits edging, troughs, and container culture where drainage is guaranteed. Plant in gritty mix and keep water minimal once roots take hold. Shear the short white spikes after bloom to hold its tight cushion shape.

planting.note: Plant rooted stock after frost in full sun with excellent drainage.

directions: A favorite for cut and dried bunches, its dark blue-purple buds hold color exceptionally well after drying. Plant in poor, alkaline soil and resist rich feeding, which weakens the scent. Harvest spikes for bundles just before the flowers fully open.

planting.note: Set out after frost into lean, gritty, sun-baked ground.

directions: Known for plump pale-lilac buds and a strong sweet fragrance. Plant where afternoon sun and dry roots keep foliage healthy. Cut the fat spikes early for potpourri, when color and oils peak.

planting.note: Plant after frost into full sun and free-draining soil.

directions: A compact deep-blue selection well suited to low hedging and knot gardens. Keep the base open to air and avoid crowding to reduce mildew in damp regions. Shear lightly each spring to maintain a rounded, dense habit.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into a hot, dry, well-drained site.

directions: Similar to Hidcote but with silvery foliage and long dark-purple spikes. Excellent for drying, holding both scent and color. Give lean soil and full exposure, and prune yearly to prevent a woody, splayed base.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun with sharp drainage.

directions: This vigorous Dutch form throws long spikes that radiate outward, giving a fountain-like display. Plant in poor, gritty soil and water sparingly once established. Cut spikes for bouquets as the lower florets begin to open.

planting.note: Set out after frost into gritty, well-drained soil.

directions: A dwarf pale pink-and-cream form ideal for pots and front-of-border edging. Ensure fast drainage and full sun to keep the small mound compact. Trim after flowering to prevent it from opening up in the center.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a sunny, sharply drained bed.

directions: One of the deepest true-pink English lavenders, forming a rounded fragrant bush. Give it lean soil and avoid winter wet, which is its main weakness. Shear off spent spikes to tidy the plant and prompt more bloom.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into full sun and free-draining ground.

directions: An early-blooming compact form with violet-blue spikes and a clean fragrance. Plant in gritty, alkaline soil and keep the roots on the dry side. Harvest early for one of the first lavender crops of the season.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun with excellent drainage.

directions: A rounded, symmetrical bush covered in short, fat dark-blue spikes. Its tidy dome shape needs little pruning to look neat. Give lean soil and full sun, and cut spikes for drying at peak color.

planting.note: Set out after frost in a hot, dry, sunny position.

directions: Valued for reliable rebloom, often producing a strong second flush in autumn. Plant in fast-draining soil and shear after the first bloom to trigger the encore. The bicolor blue-and-violet spikes are excellent for fresh cutting.

planting.note: Plant after frost into lean, gritty soil in full sun.

directions: A soft pale-pink counterpart to the classic blue Hidcote, compact and neat. Give the same dry, alkaline conditions to keep foliage silvery and healthy. Deadhead after bloom to preserve the tidy cushion form.

planting.note: Start seed indoors about two months before the last frost for first-year bloom.

directions: This seed strain flowers the first year if started early indoors under bright light. Surface-sow and keep just moist until sprouts appear, then grow cool and airy. Transplant after frost and shear spent spikes to prompt repeat bloom.

planting.note: Plant after frost into hot, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: The most widely grown oil lavandin, prized for its heavy fragrance and long silver-purple spikes. Space generously for airflow and plant in poor, gritty soil. Cut the wands when the lowest florets open for the strongest oil and lasting dried color.

planting.note: Transplant after frost in full sun with excellent drainage.

directions: Famous for long, thin light-purple spikes that dry beautifully and shed easily for sachets. Plant in lean, sharply drained soil and give plenty of room. Harvest wands just before full bloom for the best drying quality.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun; tolerates humidity better than most.

directions: Bred for outstanding cold and humidity tolerance and strong resistance to root and foliar diseases. It forms a uniform silver mound topped with violet-blue spikes. Give full sun and good drainage, and shear after bloom to keep the dome dense.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun with fast drainage.

directions: A large, bold lavandin with thick stems and big fat purple flower heads. Its size makes a dramatic specimen or tall informal hedge. Plant in poor, dry soil and prune hard each spring to control its vigor.

planting.note: Plant after frost into hot, dry, sharply drained soil.

directions: Grown as much for its striking woolly silver-white foliage as its lavender-blue spikes. The bright leaves stay attractive through the season. Give it lean soil and full sun, and shear yearly to maintain the tidy mound.

planting.note: Transplant after frost in full sun with excellent drainage.

directions: A white-flowered lavandin whose clean spikes glow against silver foliage. Plant it among blue lavenders for striking contrast in a formal bed. Give dry, gritty soil and full sun, cutting spikes for pale dried arrangements.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a sunny, sharply drained site.

directions: A robust, broad-mounded lavandin with gray-green foliage and blue-violet spikes. It blooms late and holds up well in the landscape as a large hedge. Provide poor, dry soil and full sun, pruning firmly each spring.

planting.note: Plant after frost into lean, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: A tall, vigorous English lavandin with very long stems and pale purple flowers. Its height makes a bold backdrop or screening hedge. Give room and dry soil, and cut the long wands for large dried bundles.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun with sharp drainage.

directions: A commercial oil lavandin with a softer, more English-like fragrance than Grosso. It forms a rounded silvery bush with abundant violet spikes. Plant in dry, gritty soil and harvest at first bloom for distilling or drying.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun with good drainage.

directions: A popular compact Spanish lavender with plum-purple heads topped by pale violet ears. It blooms heavily in spring and is excellent in containers. Deadhead often and shear lightly to encourage repeated flushes.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun with sharp drainage.

directions: Distinctive for its cerise-pink flower heads crowned with pale pink ears. The unusual coloring stands out in pots and warm borders. Provide gritty, well-drained soil and deadhead to extend the long bloom season.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun with excellent drainage.

directions: A vigorous West Coast favorite with rich purple heads and long violet ears. It flowers profusely and tolerates heat and drought once established. Shear after each flush and give lean, dry soil in full sun.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun with fast drainage.

directions: Named for its long white ears that flush pink and purple as they age. The color-shifting bracts give a lively, animated look over the flower heads. Grow in gritty soil and full sun, deadheading to keep new heads coming.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun with good drainage.

directions: Bears large deep-purple heads with bold, showy matching ears. The big flowers make it one of the more dramatic Spanish lavenders. Give sharp drainage and full sun, and shear spent heads for a long display.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun with sharp drainage.

directions: An early, free-blooming series selection with rounded purple heads and neat ears. Its compact habit suits containers and small sunny beds. Deadhead frequently and grow lean and dry for the heaviest flowering.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun with sharp drainage.

directions: A broad-leaved Mediterranean lavender with a sharp, camphor-rich fragrance used in spike oil. It blooms later than English lavender on branching stems. Give hot, dry, gritty conditions and full sun for the strongest scent.

planting.note: Plant after frost; treat as a tender perennial or annual in cold zones.

directions: Distinguished by lacy, fern-like foliage and blue-violet flowers on branched, forked stems. It blooms fast from seed and flowers over a very long season in warmth. Grow in gritty soil and full sun, and shelter or overwinter it from frost.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun with excellent drainage.

directions: An unusual Spanish-type lavender with bright green foliage and pale greenish-white flower heads topped by white ears. Its lemony resinous scent sets it apart from other lavenders. Grow in sharply drained, slightly acidic soil and full sun.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a hot, dry spot with perfect drainage.

directions: Prized for thick woolly white foliage that looks almost felted year-round. It bears deep violet spikes on tall stems in summer. It resents damp above all, so give it the sharpest drainage and full baking sun.

planting.note: Plant after frost; grow as a tender perennial or annual where cold.

directions: A Canary Islands species with soft silvery divided foliage and long branched stems of blue-violet flowers. It blooms almost continuously in frost-free gardens or as a summer container plant. Give gritty soil and full sun and protect from cold.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost in full sun with sharp drainage.

directions: A large, sterile hybrid with broad toothed leaves and tall spikes, grown only from cuttings. It reaches shrub size in mild climates and flowers over a long warm season. Give room, lean soil, and full sun, pruning to keep it shapely.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun with excellent drainage.

directions: A hybrid of English and woolly lavender combining silvery foliage with large violet spikes. The bright silver leaves stay handsome through the season. Grow in gritty, dry soil and full sun, shearing after bloom to keep the mound neat.

planting.note: Start seed indoors well before the last frost for first-year flowers.

directions: A compact seed strain that blooms the first season with rich dark-purple spikes. Surface-sow under bright light and grow cool for sturdy plants. Transplant after frost into dry, gritty soil and shear spent spikes to prompt rebloom.

planting.note: Set divisions out in spring after the soil warms and drains freely.

directions: Plant this sturdy tall selection with the crown level to the soil so the stems stay upright without staking. Give it steady summer moisture and cut clumps back after bloom to keep them tidy. Long-lasting single white daisies open from midsummer and hold well as cut flowers.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter or divide established clumps in early spring.

directions: Press seed onto the surface and keep it moist and lit until it germinates in a couple of weeks. This cold-tough classic wants full sun and well-drained soil, and rewards deadheading with a longer run of bloom. Broad single white daisies appear across summer and make reliable cut flowers.

planting.note: Plant compact divisions in spring at the front or middle of a border.

directions: Set this dwarf selection near the border edge where its short, self-supporting stems show off. Water evenly through the heat and shear off finished flowers to encourage repeat bloom. Bright white single daisies cover the compact mound from early to midsummer.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter for bloom the first year in mild zones.

directions: Surface-sow and keep bright and moist until seedlings emerge, then grow on before transplanting. Expect fringed, twisted, and frilled white petals that vary from plant to plant for an informal look. Shaggy semi-double blooms open through summer and last well when cut.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Set divisions with the crown at soil level and mulch lightly to conserve moisture. This heirloom bears frilled, semi-double white flowers with a soft fringed edge. Deadhead regularly and enjoy repeat flushes from early summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant nursery divisions in spring in full sun with good drainage.

directions: Set this compact selection at the crown level and water steadily during establishment. Flowers open lemon-yellow and fade to soft cream, giving a two-tone effect on one plant. The tidy mound blooms heavily from early summer with regular deadheading.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; well suited to containers and small beds.

directions: Plant this compact, free-flowering selection where its sturdy stems need no staking. Keep it evenly moist and remove spent blooms to keep new buds coming. Classic white single daisies cover the mound from late spring through summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: Set this low, mounding selection near the border front where its soft-yellow blooms read well. Water evenly and deadhead to prolong the show. Butter-yellow single daisies fade toward creamy tones as they age through summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Plant with the crown at soil level and give it full sun for the best flower count. The blooms open pale yellow and mature to white, with a distinctive frilled inner ruff of shorter petals. Deadhead often for a long midsummer display.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun with steady moisture.

directions: Set divisions at crown level and water through establishment for strong, upright stems. This vigorous selection produces large single white daisies with broad overlapping petals. Deadhead spent flowers and enjoy repeat bloom from early summer.

planting.note: Plant compact divisions in spring for front-of-border use.

directions: Plant this very compact selection at the border edge or in a container. Keep the soil evenly moist and shear lightly after the first flush to renew the mound. Abundant white single daisies smother the low plant in early to midsummer.

common_name: Ooh La La Spider Shasta Daisy
scientific_name: Leucanthemum x superbum 'Ooh La La Spider'

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny, free-draining border.

directions: Set divisions at soil level and grow in full sun for the fullest bloom. The white ray petals are quilled and spoon-tipped, giving a spidery, fringed texture. Deadhead through the season for repeat flushes from early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun with good drainage.

directions: Plant with the crown level and water evenly during the first season. This tidy, mid-height selection carries large single white daisies on strong stems. Remove faded flowers to keep new buds forming through summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun and rich, well-drained soil.

directions: Set divisions at crown level and support tall stems if grown in a windy spot. The fully double flowers form a dense pompon of short white petals around a cushioned center. Deadhead to prolong the summer bloom and use the blooms as long-lasting cut flowers.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny, well-drained border.

directions: Plant at crown level in full sun and keep evenly watered while establishing. The narrow, threadlike white ray petals give a soft, shaggy, informal flower. Deadhead through the season for a long run of bloom from midsummer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun with steady moisture.

directions: Set this classic double selection at crown level and water through establishment. The pure white flowers are fully double and neat, excellent for cutting. Deadhead regularly to keep the compact plant blooming through summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Plant this tall double selection at crown level and give strong stems room to stand. Double white flowers show a short frilled crest at the center over a broad skirt of petals. Deadhead for a long summer display and cut freely for arrangements.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun with good drainage.

directions: Set divisions at crown level and water evenly while the clump fills in. The semi-double white flowers carry a distinct raised golden crest at the center. Deadhead spent blooms to encourage a long summer show and good cutting stems.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter for compact plants that flower the first year.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed and keep it moist and bright until it sprouts. This dwarf seed strain forms a low mound covered in single white daisies. Deadhead through the season and enjoy bloom from early summer at the front of the border.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter; this fast strain flowers the first season.

directions: Press seed onto the surface and hold it moist and warm until germination in about two weeks. This award-winning dwarf blooms quickly from seed and stays neat and compact. Bright white single daisies cover the mound all summer with deadheading.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: Set divisions at crown level and water evenly as the clump establishes. Flowers open soft yellow, then mellow to cream and finally near-white, showing all three tones at once. Deadhead to keep the color show going through summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny, free-draining border.

directions: Plant at crown level and grow in full sun for the crispest form. The flowers show a raised, frilly golden center like a fried egg ringed by short white petals. Deadhead often for a tidy, long-flowering compact plant.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun with good drainage.

directions: Set divisions at soil level and water through establishment for full flowering. The narrow, rolled white petals give a quilled, spidery look on a compact plant. Deadhead through the season for repeat bloom from early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: Plant at crown level and give full sun for dense, upright growth. This compact selection carries large single white daisies over dark green foliage. Deadhead spent flowers to extend a heavy summer bloom.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun with steady moisture.

directions: Set this very compact selection near the border edge or in a pot. Water evenly and deadhead to keep the mound tidy and blooming. The unusual flowers show frilled, tufted white petals over a low, dense plant all summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny, well-drained border.

directions: Plant at crown level and grow in full sun with even moisture. This selection produces broad single white daisies on sturdy, self-supporting stems. Deadhead through the season for a long midsummer display and good cut flowers.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun and free-draining soil.

directions: Set divisions at crown level and water while the clump establishes. This heirloom bears double, creamy flowers flushed with a soft yellow tint. Deadhead regularly to prolong bloom and cut stems for the vase.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun with good drainage.

directions: Plant this tall, large-flowered selection at crown level and stake if exposed. The big semi-double to double white flowers have frilled, ruffled petals. Deadhead to keep the long summer bloom coming and cut for arrangements.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Set divisions at crown level and grow in full sun for compact, sturdy growth. The white flowers carry twisted, quilled petals for a soft, fringed look. Deadhead through the season to keep the plant flowering into fall.

planting.note: Sow directly in spring or fall; it naturalizes readily in meadows.

directions: Scatter seed on cleared ground in spring or fall and rake in lightly, keeping it moist until it sprouts. This tough wild daisy tolerates poor soil and light drought once established. Simple white daisies with yellow centers bloom from late spring through summer; deadhead to limit self-seeding where it may spread.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun with good drainage.

directions: Set divisions at crown level and water evenly as the clump fills in. The narrow, threadlike white petals give an airy, fringed daisy on upright stems. Deadhead through summer for a long, informal display.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: Plant this large, tall selection at crown level and give it room to stand. It bears very big single white daisies on strong stems, excellent for the back of a border. Deadhead through summer and cut the long stems for arrangements.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny, well-drained border.

directions: Set this compact, uniform selection at crown level for a neat massed effect. Water evenly and deadhead to keep the low mound flowering. Single white daisies cover the plant heavily from early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun with good drainage.

directions: Set divisions at crown level and water while establishing for strong stems. This selection carries large single white daisies with a bright yellow eye. Deadhead regularly for a long summer show and dependable cut flowers.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun and free-draining soil.

directions: Plant at crown level and grow in full sun for compact, upright form. The white flowers have quilled, spoon-tipped petals around a soft yellow center. Deadhead through summer to keep the tidy plant in continuous bloom.

planting.note: A fall sowing on sharply drained soil suits this southeastern native.

directions: Sow seed in autumn on gritty, well-drained soil and keep it lean. Its fine grassy foliage forms a tidy mound topped by slender purple wands. Bloom comes in late summer and holds well into fall on this dainty rock-garden species.

planting.note: Fall-sow on dry, rocky ground where summers run hot.

directions: Direct-sow in autumn onto lean, well-drained soil in full sun. The pointed, spreading bracts give the flower heads a distinctive spiky look. Its large purple heads open in early to midsummer, a bit earlier than most kin.

planting.note: Sow in fall so the seed overwinters and breaks dormancy on its own.

directions: Fall-sow outdoors or cold-stratify before a spring start under lights. It tolerates dry, sandy or rocky soils and open woodland edges. Loosely spaced purple heads open in late summer into early autumn.

planting.note: A fall sowing on thin, limey soil mimics its native prairie haunts.

directions: Sow seed outdoors in autumn on dry, alkaline, well-drained ground. This short, restrained species stays compact and needs no staking. Its cylinder-shaped purple heads open midsummer into early fall.

planting.note: Fall-sow on dry, well-drained ground in a hot, sunny spot.

directions: Direct-sow in autumn where the deep taproot can settle undisturbed. Extremely heat- and drought-tolerant once established, it thrives on poor limestone soils. Narrow, needle-like leaves clothe stems that bloom purple in fall.

planting.note: Sow in fall on sandy, sharply drained soil in the warm South.

directions: Direct-sow in autumn onto deep sand in full sun. Unusual pale pink to whitish bracts give this species its elegant, feathery look. It blooms in fall and is well suited to sandy coastal-plain gardens.

planting.note: Fall-sow on dry sand where southern summers stay hot.

directions: Sow seed in autumn on lean, sandy, fast-draining soil in full sun. Fine, almost thread-like leaves clothe the lower stems of this southeastern native. Slender purple spikes rise in fall above the wiry foliage.

planting.note: Fall-sow on well-drained soil at woodland edges or open glades.

directions: Sow seed outdoors in fall on dry, well-drained, rocky ground. It tolerates partial sun better than many blazing stars and grows in open uplands. Broad-based purple heads open in late summer into fall.

planting.note: Set corms out in early spring, growing point up, a few inches deep.

directions: Plant corms two to three inches deep in well-drained soil, or divide clumps in early spring. This compact selection needs no staking and suits the front of a border or a container. Deep purple spikes open top-down in mid to late summer and draw butterflies.

planting.note: Start stratified seed indoors, or plant corms in early spring.

directions: Sow chilled seed indoors and grow on before setting out after frost, or plant corms shallowly. This uniform seed strain is a favorite of cut-flower growers for its long violet spikes. Cut stems when the top few florets have opened for the longest vase life.

planting.note: Start stratified seed indoors, or set corms out in early spring.

directions: Grow stratified seed on indoors, or plant corms two to three inches deep in spring. The creamy-white spikes make an airy counterpoint to purple forms in the border. Harvest for the vase as the uppermost florets begin to open.

planting.note: Plant corms in early spring with the growing point facing up.

directions: Set corms a few inches deep in well-drained soil, or divide established clumps in spring. This white-flowered form pairs well with grasses and pale summer perennials. Its clean white spikes open from the top down in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Set corms out in early spring, a few inches deep, growing point up.

directions: Plant corms two to three inches deep in average, well-drained soil in full sun. This selection is prized for its rich violet-blue spikes and sturdy stems. The flowers open top-down in mid to late summer and are excellent for cutting.

planting.note: Plant this dwarf selection in spring into moist, rich soil or a large container.

directions: Use this compact form at the front of a shaded, damp border or in a pot kept well watered. Set the crown at soil level and never allow it to dry out fully. Small mounds of dark purple leaves send up orange flowers on short stems in summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, rich soil in part shade.

directions: Choose this tidier version of the rocket ligularia for smaller shaded, damp spots where a full-sized plant would crowd. Set the crown level with the soil and keep it consistently watered. Dark stems hold spikes of bright yellow flowers over deeply toothed foliage in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant this dwarf selection in spring in moist, rich soil or a container.

directions: Use this small, floriferous selection at the front of a shaded, damp bed or in a well-watered pot. Plant the crown level with the soil and never let it fully dry. Neat mounds of dark green leaves carry orange-yellow daisy flowers over a long summer season.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in rich, moist soil in part shade.

directions: Plant this hybrid in a moist, sheltered border where its large rounded leaves and tall flower cones have space. Keep the crown level with the soil and water freely through the growing season. Conical spikes of orange-gold daisy flowers rise above the foliage in late summer.

planting.note: Plant this compact selection in spring in moist, fertile soil.

directions: Grow this dark-leaved, well-branched selection in part shade with steadily moist soil to hold its rich color. Set the crown level with the surface and water in dry spells. Deeply cut maroon-tinted leaves are topped by orange-yellow flowers in summer.

planting.note: Start seed indoors well before the last frost and set plants out once nights stay mild.

directions: Barely cover the ridged seed and keep the tray around 70 degrees until sprouts appear in a couple of weeks. Move plants to lean, sharply drained soil in full sun and avoid overhead watering to prevent crown rot. Cut the papery, winged flower stalks for fresh or dried use when most florets on a spray have opened.

planting.note: Set young plants out in spring into a permanent, well-drained spot.

directions: Sow shallowly and expect uneven germination over several weeks at cool room temperature. Give this plant a deep, gritty soil and full sun, as its long taproot resents transplanting once established. Airy clouds of tiny lavender-blue flowers rise above leathery basal leaves in midsummer and dry beautifully.

planting.note: Plant out after frost danger passes in mild-winter regions where it grows year-round.

directions: Start seed indoors in warmth and grow on until roots fill the cell before planting into fast-draining coastal soil. This tender evergreen tolerates salt spray and drought once settled, so water sparingly. Large sprays of deep purple calyxes topped with tiny white flowers appear from spring into summer.

planting.note: Establish in spring in a sunny border where it can stay put for years.

directions: Sow into gritty compost and keep cool and evenly moist until germination, which can be slow. This tough, cold-hardy species forms a woody crown and wants full sun with excellent drainage. Misty sprays of small lavender-blue flowers open in late summer and hold their color when dried.

planting.note: Tuck small plants into a rock garden or gravel bed in spring.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed and press it into a sharply drained mix, then keep barely moist. This dwarf, mounding species thrives in full sun and lean, sandy or gravelly ground and dislikes winter wet. A haze of tiny lilac flowers on wiry, branching stems covers the low rosettes in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, seaside-style bed that stays well drained.

directions: Sow shallowly in gritty soil and be patient, as germination is often erratic. A salt-marsh native, it accepts both drought and occasional wet feet as long as it gets full sun. Flat-topped clusters of small violet flowers open in late summer above spoon-shaped basal leaves.

planting.note: Set out in spring in a sunny, salt-tolerant coastal planting.

directions: Start seed indoors in a sandy mix and keep lightly moist until seedlings emerge. This North American salt-marsh native handles salty soil and periodic flooding, so it suits difficult coastal edges in full sun. Delicate branching sprays of pale lavender flowers appear from midsummer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring along a sunny, well-drained coastal or bay margin.

directions: Sow into a fast-draining sandy medium and keep barely moist until germination. Native to Pacific coast salt marshes, it is very salt-tolerant and prefers full sun with a coarse soil. Small pale violet flowers cluster on branched stems above leathery leaves through the warm months.

planting.note: Set plants out after frost in a warm, sunny cut-flower bed.

directions: Start seed indoors in warmth and grow on until well rooted before planting into free-draining soil. Give it full sun and modest water, avoiding wet crowns. Airy sprays carry tiny white to pale yellow flowers on branching stems, prized for fresh and dried arrangements.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny trough, wall crevice, or gravel garden.

directions: Surface-sow the tiny seed onto a gritty alpine mix and press in without covering. This miniature Mediterranean cliff-dweller demands sharp drainage and full sun and hates standing moisture. Wiry stems hold small lilac flowers just above a tight rosette in early summer.

planting.note: Grow outdoors only in frost-free areas or in a large container.

directions: Sow indoors in warmth and grow on until the rosette is well developed before planting out. This tender Canary Islands species forms broad, leathery leaves and wants full sun with gritty, fast-draining soil. Tall branched panicles carry masses of tiny lilac flowers over a long season.

planting.note: Site in a frost-free garden or overwinter under glass in cold regions.

directions: Start seed in warmth and grow on for a full season to build a woody base. This shrubby Canary Islands sea lavender develops a stout stem and needs full sun with very sharp drainage. Broad, branching heads of small blue-violet flowers make a long-lasting display on established plants.

planting.note: Plant in spring into a permanent, sunny, well-drained position.

directions: Raise from seed sown shallowly and kept cool, then move to a deep, gritty soil while young to protect the taproot. This selection is valued for its especially rich violet flowers on airy panicles. Cut stems in midsummer for drying, or leave them to add long-lasting color to the border.

planting.note: Set out divisions or young plants in spring in full sun.

directions: Best increased from division or nursery plants set into deep, sharply drained soil. Give it full sun and go easy on water and feeding, which keeps the crown healthy. It produces an especially fine, misty cloud of pale lavender-blue flowers in summer that dries well for arrangements.

planting.note: Sow indoors before last frost and transplant when nights warm.

directions: Barely cover the winged seed and keep warm until seedlings emerge in a couple of weeks. This vigorous cut-flower strain offers a broad color range on long, strong stems, so give it full sun and lean soil. Harvest sprays for fresh or dried bunches once most florets are open.

planting.note: Start indoors ahead of frost and set out into warm soil.

directions: Sow shallowly at around 70 degrees and thin to strong seedlings before transplanting. This mix leans into warm tones of apricot, gold, rose, and peach, thriving in full sun and dry, well-drained ground. Cut the papery sprays for drying when the colored calyxes are fully expanded.

planting.note: Sow indoors early and transplant after the danger of frost.

directions: Press seed lightly into warm, moist mix and expect emergence within a couple of weeks. Bred for cutting, this strain produces sturdy, well-branched stems in a full spectrum of colors and rewards full sun with lean soil. Cut generously once florets open to encourage more flowering sprays.

planting.note: Start indoors before last frost for uniform cut-flower rows.

directions: Sow the winged seed shallowly and hold near 70 degrees until it germinates. This uniform, high-quality selection blooms a clear rose and grows tall and straight for cutting in full sun and dry soil. Harvest when the papery bracts are fully colored, then hang upside down to dry.

planting.note: Sow indoors early and move out once frost has passed.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep the flat warm and evenly moist until sprouting. This selection carries bright yellow bracts on long, uniform stems that suit both fresh bouquets and drying. Grow in full sun with sparing water and cut when the color is fully developed.

planting.note: Start indoors before last frost for the deepest blue cut stems.

directions: Sow shallowly and maintain warmth until seedlings appear, then grow on strong before transplanting. Prized for its rich deep-blue bracts, it holds color exceptionally well when dried. Plant in full sun and dry soil, and cut the sprays as the florets open.

planting.note: Sow indoors ahead of frost and set out into warm ground.

directions: Press seed lightly into warm mix and keep it just moist until germination. This soft apricot selection is a favorite for warm-toned bouquets and dries to a lovely muted shade. Give full sun and lean, well-drained soil, and cut stems once the bracts fully color.

planting.note: Start indoors early and transplant after frost danger ends.

directions: Sow the winged seed shallowly and keep warm until it sprouts within a couple of weeks. This clean white selection is a versatile filler for fresh and dried work on tall, straight stems. Grow in full sun with modest water and harvest when the papery bracts are fully open.

planting.note: Sow indoors before last frost and plant out into warm soil.

directions: Barely cover the seed and hold near 70 degrees until seedlings emerge. This selection delivers a saturated purple that stays vivid after drying, making it a mainstay of everlasting bouquets. Plant in full sun and dry, well-drained soil, and cut the sprays as florets open.

planting.note: Plant nursery-grown divisions in spring in a warm, sunny cut bed.

directions: This sterile hybrid is grown from nursery divisions or tissue-cultured plants rather than seed. Set it into free-draining soil in full sun and keep water modest to avoid crown rot. It sends up airy sprays of tiny white flowers on branching stems that make a superb, long-lasting cut and dried flower.

planting.note: Sow directly around the last spring frost where the plants are to stay, since the taproot dislikes moving.

directions: Scatter seed shallowly onto raked, gritty soil and press it in without burying, keeping the surface just moist until sprouts appear. Give the plants full sun and sharp drainage, and shear spent stems in midsummer to tidy the airy clumps. Sky-blue saucers open each morning and drop by afternoon from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Direct sow near the last frost in a sunny, freely draining bed.

directions: Press the fine seed lightly into a well-drained seedbed and thin the seedlings to a hand's width apart. This is a lean-soil plant, so skip rich feeding and let it bask in full sun. Pure white five-petaled flowers float above wiry blue-green stems through early summer.

planting.note: Sow in place after the frost danger passes; this compact selection suits the front of a border.

directions: Sow onto firmed soil and barely cover, keeping seedlings on the dry side once established. This dwarf form stays neat, so it needs little staking or cutting back beyond a light midsummer trim. Rich blue flowers cover the mound from late spring into midsummer.

planting.note: Direct sow into gritty soil around the last frost for bloom the following season.

directions: Sprinkle seed on the surface, tamp gently, and keep evenly moist until germination. Plant it in lean, sharply drained ground and give it full sun for the densest flowering. Clean white blooms open in a low, tidy dome through early summer.

planting.note: Sow into a raised or scree bed; this alpine form thrives in gritty, fast-draining sites.

directions: Sow onto a gritty rock-garden mix and leave uncovered, as light aids germination. Keep the crown dry in winter with a topdressing of grit to prevent rot. Loose sprays of pale blue flowers dance over the low tufts in early summer.

planting.note: Fall or early-spring sowing works well; the seed benefits from a period of cold, moist chilling.

directions: Broadcast seed in autumn or after chilling and press into firm, dry soil. This western native tolerates drought and poor ground once rooted, so water sparingly. Pale sky-blue flowers open at dawn and shed petals by afternoon from late spring through summer.

planting.note: A restoration selection best sown in fall or early spring across sunny, dry ground.

directions: Sow into loosened soil and rake in lightly, then let winter cold ready the seed for spring. Prized for reliable establishment and long flowering, it needs no fertilizer in average ground. Abundant blue blooms carry from late spring well into summer.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost into warm, well-drained soil; often the showiest of the blue flaxes.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in a sunny, gritty spot and thin to give each plant room. Enjoy its drought tolerance by watering only in prolonged dry spells. Large azure flowers with paler eyes bloom generously from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Sow in place in a hot, sharply drained bed for the deepest flower color.

directions: Press the small seed into warm soil and keep just damp until it sprouts. This selection carries unusually large, intense-blue flowers, so site it in full sun for the best display. Blooms appear over a long stretch from late spring through midsummer.

planting.note: Direct sow into lean, well-drained soil in full sun after the last frost.

directions: Sow the seed shallowly and firm it in, keeping the bed lightly moist through germination. It resents wet feet, so plant on a slope or in gritty ground and go easy on water. Clusters of glossy golden-yellow cups open in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Sow in a sunny rock garden or border edge where its low habit shows well.

directions: Sprinkle seed on gritty soil and barely cover, thinning to leave the tidy mounds room. This compact form needs sharp drainage and full sun, with grit around the crown to guard against winter wet. Bright yellow flowers smother the low cushion in early summer.

planting.note: Set out young plants in spring into a sunny scree bed; this sterile hybrid is grown from cuttings, not seed.

directions: Plant a rooted cutting into gritty, fast-draining soil with the crown proud of the surface. Because it sets no seed, propagate it from short summer cuttings and overwinter young plants under cover in colder areas. Domes of large lemon-yellow flowers cover the grey-green foliage through early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring against a warm wall or in a sheltered rock garden; it is tender where winters are hard.

directions: Grow this Cretan shrublet in very free-draining soil and give it the warmest, sunniest position you have. Propagate from semi-ripe cuttings and shelter or overwinter under glass in frost-prone gardens. Large golden-yellow flowers stud the evergreen bush from late spring onward.

planting.note: Sow into a sunny, sharply drained rock garden or raised bed.

directions: Sow seed thinly on gritty soil and press in without deep covering. This Mediterranean species wants lean ground and full sun, and it sulks in rich, damp beds. Wide funnel-shaped yellow flowers rise above grey-green rosettes in early summer.

planting.note: Sow into scree or a trough; the tight flower heads suit alpine plantings.

directions: Surface-sow onto gritty compost and leave uncovered for light to trigger germination. Keep the crown dry and provide full sun and excellent drainage. Clustered heads of bright yellow flowers top the stems in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Direct sow into dry, sunny ground; it self-sows gently once settled.

directions: Scatter seed on firmed soil and press in, thinning seedlings to space them out. Drought-tolerant and undemanding, it thrives in poor, gritty beds with full sun. Nodding buds open to airy blue flowers from late spring through summer.

planting.note: Sow or plant out in spring in a mild, sunny, well-drained spot; protect from hard frost.

directions: Sow onto free-draining soil and keep just moist while the seedlings settle. In cold regions grow it in a container that can shelter over winter, since it is not fully frost-hardy. Clean white five-petaled flowers open over slender stems through summer.

planting.note: Best in a trough or scree bed; this mat-former needs very sharp drainage.

directions: Sow into gritty alpine mix and grow the seedlings on lean before setting out. Give it full sun, a poor stony root run, and a grit collar to keep the crown dry. Pearly white flowers with pink-veined backs open over the low needle-leaved mat in summer.

planting.note: Direct sow into a sunny, well-drained bank where its soft-hairy foliage catches the light.

directions: Press seed into gritty soil and keep lightly moist until the downy seedlings emerge. It asks only for full sun and free drainage, tolerating dry, stony ground. Pale lilac-blue flowers open above the hairy grey-green stems in early summer.

planting.note: Sow in a sunny meadow-style bed with good drainage.

directions: Sow the fine seed shallowly and thin the sticky-leaved seedlings once they take hold. Unusual among flaxes for its color, it wants full sun and soil that never stays soggy. Soft pink flowers veined with darker lines open over the summer.

planting.note: Suited to a limy scree or rock garden in full sun.

directions: Sow onto gritty, lime-rich soil and leave the seed near the surface. This wiry subshrub thrives on poor, dry ground and dislikes rich feeding. Pale pink to lilac-white flowers rise on thread-fine stems in early to midsummer.

planting.note: A choice alpine best grown in a trough or scree bed in full sun.

directions: Surface-sow onto gritty compost and grow the seedlings cool and lean. Provide sharp drainage, full sun, and a dry crown through winter to keep the tight cushions healthy. Neat golden-yellow flowers cluster just above the low tuft in early summer.

planting.note: Start indoors early for first-season bloom and transplant after frost danger passes.

directions: Sow seed on the surface of moist mix in a warm, bright spot and mist to keep it damp. These vigorous garden hybrids want steady moisture and full sun to partial shade for the best spikes. Cut back spent stems to tidy the clump and enjoy the tall show of red, rose, or purple bloom through summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into rich, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: This sterile cross is grown from divisions, so set clumps out in spring with the crown at soil level. Give it consistently moist, fertile ground and full sun for the tallest, sturdiest spikes. Long wands of violet-purple flowers rise above bronzed foliage from midsummer into fall.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and plant out after frost; mulch the crown in cold winters.

directions: Surface sow the fine seed and keep it in bright warmth until seedlings appear. This bold Chilean species forms a large clump of grey-green leaves and wants full sun with shelter from wind. Curious tubular brick-red flowers appear on towering stems in summer; protect the crown with a deep mulch where winters are hard.

planting.note: Plant rooted divisions in spring in a warm, well-drained bed.

directions: Set divisions into well-drained soil in a sunny, sheltered spot where the clump can spread by runners. Unlike its bog-loving relatives, this species tolerates drier conditions once established. Slender orange-and-yellow tubular flowers dangle from arching stems over a long spring-to-summer season.
